topic_scope.detail: Topic headline 'Bayes theorem intuition — updating belief with new evidence' is well respected. No formula derivation or algebraic proof appears — §4 and §5.3 explicitly state the learner is not expected to derive or prove the formula. Bayes' theorem is presented as a named equation only. Base rate fallacy is mentioned as an unnamed 'reasoning error you will encounter later' — not named, not defined, correctly deferred. Conjugate priors not mentioned. Full Bayesian inference pipeline not mentioned. LLM probability distributions appear in exactly one sentence in §7 with an explicit deferral to topic 7.4. §5.5 ('conditional probability reorganised') stays within the scope of showing conceptual equivalence without teaching new advanced material.

findings.detail: The parenthetical '(marginal probability of B)' in the P(B) table row uses a term not previously defined in this corpus or in prior topics. Soft finding — the label is parenthetical only and the concept P(B) is fully explained by the surrounding prose.

beginner_readability.detail: Prior, posterior, likelihood, and Bayesian updating are each defined clearly on first use with plain-language definitions preceding formal notation. LLM is spelled out on first use ('LLM (Large Language Model)'). No paragraph introduces 3+ new terms simultaneously. Sentence length is well-controlled throughout. No hedging phrases detected. Running rain-and-clouds example provides progressive concreteness. One soft finding: 'marginal probability' appears undefined as a parenthetical table label.

'marginal probability' used as parenthetical label in §5.3 table without definition. Not a taught concept — parenthetical only. Low risk.

verdict: PASS — 2 soft findings (word count ~2445 below 3000-word standard floor but within 30% hard-fail threshold; 'marginal probability' used as undefined parenthetical in §5.3 table). All hard checks pass: required sections present and non-trivial, primary citation [1] cited 10+ times with ≥80% factual-claim coverage in §5/§6/§7, no forward references beyond prior-topic concepts, no scope creep (no derivation, base rate fallacy unnamed-and-deferred, LLM distributions deferred to 7.4), beginner readability good with all key terms defined on first use.
